1: 'Be strong (endunamou) in the grace that is in Christ Jesus' — the imperative endunamou (strengthen yourself; passive-deponent: allow yourself to be strengthened) uses the same verb as Ephesians 6:10 (be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might) and Philippians 4:13 (I can do all things through him who strengthens me). The source of strength is specified: 'the grace that is in Christ Jesus' — not Timothy's natural courage or character but the divine enabling that comes through union with Christ. The command to be strong is simultaneously a command to draw on a resource outside himself.
2: 'What you heard from me in the presence of many witnesses, entrust (parathou) to faithful people who will be able to teach others also' — the four-generation transmission: Paul → Timothy (many witnesses) → faithful people → others. This is the Pastoral Epistles' programmatic statement of apostolic succession by teaching, not by office alone. The 'many witnesses' (dia pollōn martyrōn) at which Timothy received Paul's teaching may refer to Timothy's ordination (1 Timothy 4:14; 2 Timothy 1:6) or to the broader public character of Paul's teaching. The parathēkē (deposit, entrust) vocabulary connects to 2 Timothy 1:14: what Timothy received as a deposit, he is to entrust to others as a deposit.
3: 'Share in suffering (synkakopathēson) as a good soldier (kalos stratiōtēs) of Christ Jesus' — the military metaphor inaugurates a triad of analogies (soldier, athlete, farmer) drawn from the standard Roman world of competition and labor. The Roman soldier's identity was defined by his willingness to endure hardship in service of the general; kakopathia (suffering hardship) was the soldier's expected mode of existence on campaign. Paul applies this model consistently: he himself is 'chained like a criminal' (2 Timothy 2:9) but the word of God is not chained; suffering is the expected mode of apostolic existence, not an aberration.
4: 'No soldier is entangled in civilian affairs (tais tou biou pragmateiais), wanting to please the one who enlisted him' — Roman military service required soldiers to focus exclusively on military duties; they could not engage in trade, business, or other civilian pursuits while under arms. The 'one who enlisted him' (to stratologēsanti) is the commanding officer to whom the soldier owes undivided loyalty. The application to Timothy's pastoral ministry: civilian entanglements (business, household conflicts, social obligations) should not distract from single-minded service to the one who enlisted him for ministry.
5: 'An athlete also is not crowned (stephanoutai) unless he competes according to the rules (nomimōs)' — the Greco-Roman athletic games (the Isthmian games near Corinth, the Olympic games, the Pythian games at Delphi) required competitors to follow the established rules of their sport; a rule-violator was disqualified from the prize even if he outpaced all others. Paul's athletic metaphor runs through his letters (1 Corinthians 9:24-27; Philippians 3:14; Hebrews 12:1-2); here it is applied to the rule-bound character of Christian ministry — not any method or approach, but the divinely prescribed pattern of sound teaching.
6: 'The hardworking farmer (geōrgon kopionēta) ought to receive the first share of the crops' — the agricultural proverb concludes the triad: soldier (endure hardship), athlete (compete lawfully), farmer (labor hard and receive the reward). The farmer who works hardest in planting and cultivation appropriately receives the first and best share of the harvest. Applied to Timothy: the minister who invests most fully in his work will receive the first share of the eschatological harvest. The proverb also grounds ministerial support (echoing 1 Timothy 5:17-18) in agricultural justice: labor deserves its reward.
7: 'Think over what I say, for the Lord will give you understanding in everything' (synesis en pasin) — the three analogies (soldier, athlete, farmer) are presented without Paul explicitly applying them; the application is left for Timothy to work out: 'think over what I say.' The Lord's giving of understanding is the divine pedagogy: the Spirit illuminates what the apostle presents; Timothy's reflection is the human side of a process that has divine assistance. Paul's teaching method here is suggestive rather than exhaustive — he trusts Timothy's capacity to grasp the implications.
8: 'Remember Jesus Christ, risen from the dead, of the seed of David — this is my gospel' — the summation of Paul's gospel in one sentence: resurrection and Davidic messiahship. The resurrection comes first (risen, ēgeiromenon, perfect passive participle — permanently in a risen state) before the genealogy; the historical order is inverted because the resurrection is the hermeneutical key that unlocks the significance of the Davidic descent. Paul's gospel is explicitly 'my gospel' (kata to euangelion mou) — the apostolic form of the Christ-announcement that he was commissioned to preach.
9: 'For which I am suffering to the point of being chained like a criminal (hos kakourgos), but the word of God is not chained (ou dedetai)' — kakourgos (evildoer, criminal) is the same word used of the two criminals crucified with Jesus (Luke 23:32, 33, 39). The comparison is deliberately chosen: Paul is chained with the classification given to criminals, just as the Lord he proclaims was crucified between criminals. The chain-paradox: the apostle is bound but the word that he proclaims is unbound, continuing its work through other preachers and through the letter itself.
10: 'Therefore I endure all things for the sake of the elect (eklektous), so that they also may obtain salvation in Christ Jesus with eternal glory' — the elect who do not yet know their election are the missionary horizon: Paul's suffering is in service of those who will eventually come to faith through his gospel and its transmission. The phrase 'in Christ Jesus with eternal glory' (meta doxēs aiōniou) places the eschatological goal alongside the present suffering, as in Romans 8:18 (the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing to the glory that is to be revealed).
11: 'If we died with him, we will also live with him' — the trustworthy saying (the fifth of the Pastoral Epistles' pistoi logoi) is a confessional couplet that may derive from early Christian baptismal liturgy. The dying-with-Christ language is the same as Romans 6:3-8 (we were buried with him by baptism into death) and Galatians 2:20 (I have been crucified with Christ). The couplet's four statements (live/reign/deny/faithless) move from the assured positive (life and reign) to the conditional warning (denial leads to denial) to the unconditional divine faithfulness that stands even when human faith fails.
12: 'If we endure, we will also reign with him; if we deny him, he will also deny us' — the endurance-reign pairing echoes the Beatitudes' reversal logic (Matthew 5:10-12) and the promise to those who endure in Revelation 2:26; 2 Timothy 3:21 (the one who overcomes, I will grant to sit with me on my throne). The denial-warning recalls Matthew 10:33 (whoever denies me before men, I will also deny before my Father who is in heaven) and Peter's denial-restoration narrative, which stands behind the Petrine tradition as the cautionary example of denial that grace overcomes.
13: 'If we are faithless, he remains faithful, for he cannot deny himself' — the final line of the hymn is the theological climax: God's faithfulness is not conditional on human faithfulness. Even when the human partner in the covenant is faithless (apistousmen), the divine covenant partner remains faithful (pistos menei). The basis: 'he cannot deny himself' — God's faithfulness is a predicate of his own identity, not a response to human performance. This is not a permission for faithlessness; it is the bedrock of confidence for those who have denied him and yet return (as Peter did).
14: 'Remind them of these things, charging them before God not to quarrel about words (logomachein)' — logomacheo (word-battle) appears only here in the NT; the Stoic tradition criticized pointless verbal disputes as a mark of bad philosophy. The Hellenistic rhetorical tradition distinguished between substantive debate (dialektikē) and mere verbal sparring. Paul's charge 'before God' (enōpion tou theou) frames the instruction with the gravity of divine witness: word-battles are not merely unpleasant but actively destructive of those who hear them, and they occur in the presence of the one who holds the ministers accountable.
16: 'Avoid irreverent empty chatter (bebēlous kenophōnias), for it will lead people into more and more ungodliness' — kenophōnia (empty talk, vain noise) is a compound of kenos (empty) and phōnē (voice/sound): vocal activity that lacks substance. The trajectory is downward: 'more and more ungodliness' (epi pleion asebeia) — the progressive moral deterioration that empty religious discourse produces. The false teachers in Ephesus created a feedback loop: their speculation promoted controversy, the controversy increased ungodliness, which promoted further controversy. Sound teaching reverses the loop.
17: 'Their talk will spread like gangrene (gangraina)' — gangraina (gangrene) is a medical term used by Hippocrates and Galen for the progressive tissue death that spreads from an infected wound. The medical metaphor frames the false teaching as a disease: physically localized at first (Hymenaeus and Philetus), it spreads through the body of the community if not treated. Hymenaeus appears in 1 Timothy 1:20 (handed to Satan for blasphemy); Philetus is otherwise unknown. Their teaching has moved from Ephesus (1 Timothy 1:20) to wherever Paul is writing (the false teaching has metastasized).
18: 'Who have swerved from the truth, saying that the resurrection has already happened (anastasis ēdē gegonen), and they are upsetting the faith of some' — the 'realized eschatology' heresy: the resurrection is already past, meaning that what believers expect is already accomplished in some spiritual sense. This collapses the future bodily resurrection (1 Cor 15) into a present spiritual experience, denying the eschatological remainder. The teaching may have been influenced by Gnostic spiritualizing of bodily resurrection; Polycarp (Phil. 7.1) later identifies denial of bodily resurrection as a fundamental error. The pastoral damage: 'upsetting the faith of some' (anatrepousin tēn tōn pisteōn) — the word anatrepō (to overturn) is used of capsizing boats.
19: 'God's solid foundation stands (ho stereos themelios tou theou hestēken)' — the building metaphor responds to the upsetting of faith by the false teachers (2 Timothy 2:18): what the false teachers overturn is not the foundation but only the unstable structure built on it. The foundation bears two inscriptions (like a foundation stone with dedicatory inscriptions): 'The Lord knows those who are his' (Numbers 16:5, where Moses says to Korah's rebels that the Lord knows those who are his) and 'Let everyone who names the name of the Lord depart from iniquity' (Isaiah 26:13 or Numbers 16:26). The double inscription combines divine sovereignty and human responsibility.
20: 'In a great house there are vessels not only of gold and silver but also of wood and clay, some for honorable use and some for dishonorable' — the household-vessel metaphor operates in a stratified social setting: wealthy Roman houses (and the large house churches that met in them) contained vessels of multiple materials and purposes. The contrast between eis timēn (for honorable use) and eis atimian (for dishonorable use) mirrors the social stratification of the household — the silver serving dish vs. the clay chamber pot. The question for the minister is not which category they were born into but which they become by their own cleansing.
21: 'If anyone cleanses himself from what is dishonorable, he will be a vessel for honorable use, set apart as holy, useful to the master of the house, ready for every good work' — the application of the vessel metaphor: the choice of what kind of vessel to be is partly within the person's power. The 'cleansing' (ekkatharsē) required echoes the purification rituals of Mosaic law applied now to moral-doctrinal separation from false teaching. The 'master of the house' (despotē) is God; being 'useful to the master' frames ministry as household stewardship rather than independent professional expertise.
22: 'Flee youthful passions (neōterikas epithymias) and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace' — neōterikos (youthful, characteristic of youth) describes the passions not only of sexual desire but of ambition, argumentativeness, and the need to win — the drives that make young leaders vulnerable to controversy-seeking and the kind of word-battles Paul has just condemned. The pursuit-list (righteousness, faith, love, peace) echoes 1 Timothy 6:11's similar list for Timothy; both passages define the character of the faithful minister by what he runs toward, not only what he flees from.
23: 'Have nothing to do with foolish, ignorant controversies (mōras kai apaideutos zētēseis); you know that they breed quarrels' — apaideutas (uneducated, uncultured) dismisses the controversies as belonging to those who have not received the proper formation. The Stoic tradition used the same term for those whose reasoning is unformed. Paul's description of the controversies as both 'foolish' (intellectually empty) and 'uneducated' (methodologically undisciplined) doubly disqualifies them: they lack both content and form. The consequence — 'they breed quarrels' — is empirically observable: controversy begets controversy in an escalating cycle.
24: 'The Lord's servant (doulon kyriou) must not be quarrelsome but kind to everyone, able to teach, patiently enduring evil (anexikakon)' — anexikakon is a compound adjective (only here in the NT) from anecho (to bear with) and kakos (evil/bad): bearing evil with patience rather than retaliating. The 'Lord's servant' title (doulos kyriou) echoes the Isaianic Servant of YHWH (Isaiah 42:1; 2 Timothy 49:6; 2 Timothy 52:13; 2 Timothy 53:11) and the Mosaic designation 'servant of God' (Deuteronomy 34:5; Joshua 1:1); applying it to Timothy places his pastoral role in the prophetic-servant tradition.
25: 'Correcting his opponents with gentleness (en praytēti paideúonta), God perhaps granting them repentance leading to a knowledge of the truth' — the correction of opponents is styled as the teacher's pedagogy, not the judge's condemnation: paideuō is to educate, to form, not to punish. The 'perhaps' (mēpote) introduces the divine element: repentance is not produced by the teacher's technique but by God's gift. The goal of correction is the opponent's repentance leading to 'knowledge of the truth' — the Pastoral Epistles' standard phrase for reception of the gospel.
26: 'And they may come to their senses and escape from the snare of the devil, who has captured them to do his will' — the 'coming to their senses' (ananēpsōsin) uses a term that evokes recovery from drunkenness or stupor: the opponents are not merely wrong but captivated in a condition that impairs judgment. The devil's 'snare' (pagida tou diabolou) — the same image as 1 Timothy 3:7 — describes the entrapment that requires divine rescue to escape. The opponents are simultaneously agents (they choose error) and victims (they are captured by the devil's agenda); pastoral correction engages both dimensions.
15: The instruction to present oneself as ‘a worker who rightly handles (orthotomounta) the word of truth’ draws on the craftsman’s metaphor: the road-builder who cuts a straight path, the carpenter who cuts true joints, or the surgeon who cuts along the correct line. The concern for accurate handling of the apostolic tradition reflects the Pastoral Epistles’ consistent anxiety about the false teachers’ distortion of the gospel. The approved worker (dokimon) is one who has passed the scrutiny of divine examination — the same root as the civic dokimasia (examination of public officials before taking office). The combination of divine approval (parastēsai tō theō) and public non-shame (anepaiskunton) covers both the vertical (God’s assessment) and horizontal (community witness) dimensions of faithful ministry.
